title: 3D Printing Rock Art Panel via Photogrammetry
author: Nur Ihsan

steps.title: Place Coded Markers (GCPs)
steps.instruction: Position the GCP on the rock art panel, ensuring an even distribution along the panel's outer rim. Employ an adhesive that leaves no residue on the panel but is sufficiently strong to secure the marker during the image capture process. Refrain from positioning the marker on or too close to the rock paintings.

steps.title: Set Up Total Station
steps.instruction: Ideally, establish the total station using the geodetic datum if one is already available at the site. If not, utilize the local coordinate system and perform the necessary calibration.

steps.title: Set Up LED Lights
steps.instruction: In cases where natural light inadequately illuminates the panel, position several LED lights to evenly light the rock art panel. Adjust the light intensity to prevent shadow casting on the panel.

steps.title: Record Marker Coordinates
steps.instruction: Document the coordinates of the marker using the total station and store it as 'target n ', where ' n ' represents the coded marker's number.

steps.title: Capture Inner Panel Coordinates
steps.instruction: Record coordinates at select points within the panel's inner area, prioritizing points with clear and sharp edges. Remember to photograph the point while the total station's laser is still active. This photograph will assist in accurately positioning the GCP during photogrammetry image processing.

steps.title: Camera Setup (Overview)
steps.instruction: Review the following camera settings before proceeding.

steps.title: Set Camera Focal Length
steps.instruction: If using a zoom lens, fix the focal length to its shortest distance. Maintain this focal length across all images.

steps.title: Configure Image File Format
steps.instruction: Configure the camera to capture images in JPEG format at the highest resolution available. For best results with highest resolution and wider color range, shoot with RAW format (NEF, CR2, PEF, etc.), as most of the photogrammetry tutorial suggested. This setting will also be beneficial if you want to reliably document the rock art panel for the purpose of general preservation. However, it will require us to do a preprocessing step such as convert the RAW files to JPEG in the next section. Also, the color accuracy is not necessarily needed since the painting will be presented not in digital format and the color reproduction will rely on the paint mixture by the artist. Furthermore, the level of detail that we get from this processing will most likely get destroyed in the subsequent steps in the 3D modelling section.

steps.title: Set Camera Shooting Mode
steps.instruction: Use Manual mode to prevent exposure level variations. Alternatively, use Aperture mode with a minimum speed of 1/80s.

steps.title: Set Camera Aperture
steps.instruction: Set the aperture between f/5.6 and f/16, with f/8 typically being the optimal setting. This increases the depth of field and minimizes diffraction, which often occurs at smaller apertures. However, the appropriate aperture level largely depends on the lens quality.

steps.title: Set Camera Speed and ISO
steps.instruction: Setting the speed can be challenging due to low light conditions, which may necessitate slower speeds and potentially introduce hand tremor. While a tripod can help stabilize the camera, cave topography may render it impractical. Therefore, setting the ISO to 640 and speed at 1/80s should yield acceptable results.

steps.title: Plan Shooting Scenario (Overview)
steps.instruction: Review the following guidelines for capturing images.

steps.title: Plan Photography Route
steps.instruction: Plan the shooting scenario before commencing photography. Depending on the panel and cave floor topography, begin by taking pictures parallel to the panel surface.

steps.title: Capture Vertical Overlap Images
steps.instruction: From the starting point, capture images in a vertical direction from the top to the bottom of the panel, maintaining a 75-85% vertical overlap between photos.

steps.title: Capture Side Overlap Images
steps.instruction: Move one step in the planned direction, then start photographing from the bottom and move upward, ensuring an 85-90% side overlap with the previous photo series.

steps.title: Complete Photography & Retrieve Markers
steps.instruction: Repeat this process until the entire panel surface is covered. Upon completion, remember to retrieve all markers from the panel.

steps.title: Transfer Images to Computer
steps.instruction: Transfer all images from the memory card to a new folder on your computer. If your computer uses an SSD for the operating system and an HDD for storage, ensure that the images are stored on the same drive as the operating system to prevent processing bottlenecks due to differing transfer rates between SSD and HDD.

steps.title: Import Images into Agisoft Metashape
steps.instruction: Launch Agisoft Metashape and import all images into the workspace as a single chunk. This can be done by selecting 'Workflow' > 'Add Photos'.

steps.title: Save Project in Agisoft Metashape
steps.instruction: Save the project in the root directory of the image folder by clicking the 'Save' button and assigning an appropriate file name.

steps.title: Estimate & Filter Image Quality
steps.instruction: Right-click on one of the photos in the Photos panel, select 'Estimate Image Quality' from the dropdown menu, and choose 'All Cameras' in the pop-up window. After processing, the Quality Column in the Photos pane will display numerical values representing the focus quality of each image. Disable any image with a quality value less than 0.5 units to exclude it from subsequent processes, as it could affect alignment quality.
